# Lecture 6gm/ID

Copyright © 2011 by Bernhard Boser

Copyright © 2013 by Boris Murmann

Copyright © 2015 by Mirco Meiners

# Transistor Sizing



* Typical problem
  * Want to realize a certain amount of gm
  * Need to determine W\, L\, ITAIL
* Classical square\-law equations are very inaccurate for modern technologies


Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# The Problem

Since there is a disconnect between actual transistor behavior and the simple square law model\, any square\-law driven design optimization will be far off from Spice results

Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# The Solution

Use pre\-computed SPICE data in hand calculations

Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Simulation Data

__% data stored in /usr/class/ee315a/__  __matlab__

__>> __  __load __  __180nch\.mat;__

__>> nch__

__nch__  __ __  __= __

__     __  __ID: \[4\-D double\]__

__     VT: \[4\-D double\]__

__     GM: \[4\-D double\]__

__    GMB: \[4\-D double\]__

__    GDS: \[4\-D double\]__

__    CGG: \[4\-D double\]__

__    CGS: \[4\-D double\]__

__    CGD: \[4\-D double\]__

__    CGB: \[4\-D double\]__

__    CDD: \[4\-D double\]__

__    CSS: \[4\-D double\]__

__    VGS: \[73x1 double\]__

__    VDS: \[73x1 double\]__

__     VS: \[11x1 double\]__

__      L: \[22x1 double\]__

__      W: 5__

__>> size\(nch\.ID\)__

__ans__  __ =__

__    __  __22    73    73    11__

Stored in a math application\, e\.g\. Matlab\, Gnu Octave or Python

Four dimensional array

Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Lookup Function (For Convenience)

__>> __  __lookup\(nch\, 'ID'\, 'VGS'\, __  __0\.5\, __  __'VDS'\, __  __0\.5\)__

__ans__  __ __  __=__

__  __  __8\.4181e\-006__

__>> __  __help __  __lookup__

__The __  __function "lookup" extracts a desired subset from the __  __4\-dimensional simulation data\. The __  __function interpolates when the requested points lie off the simulation __  __grid\.__

__  There are three basic usage modes:__

__  \(1\) Simple lookup of parameters at given \(L\, VGS\, VDS\, VS\)__

__  \(2\) Lookup of __  __arbitrary __  __ratios of parameters\, e\.g\. GM\_ID\, GM\_CGG at __  __given__  __      \(L\, VGS__  __\, VDS\, VS\)__

__  \(3\) Cross\-lookup of one ratio against another\, e\.g\. GM\_CGG for some GM\_ID__

__  In usage scenarios \(1\) and \(2\) the input parameters \(L\, VGS\, VDS\, VS\) can be __

__  listed in any order and default to the following values when not specified__  __:__

__  L = min\(__  __data\.L__  __\); \(minimum length used in simulation\)__

__  VGS = data\.VGS; \(VGS vector used during simulation\)__

__  VDS = max\(__  __data\.VDS__  __\)/2; \(VDD/2\)__

__  VS = 0;__

Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Figures of Merit for Design



* Transconductance efficiency
  * Want large gm\, for as little current as possible
* Transit frequency
  * Want large gm\, without large Cgg
* Intrinsic gain
  * Want large gm\, but no go


Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Design Tradeoff: gm/ID and fT

Moderate Inversion

Strong Inversion

Weak inversion: Large gm/ID \(>20 S/A\)\, but small fT

Strong inversion: Small gm/ID \(<10 S/A\)\, but large fT

Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Product of gm/ID and fT

Moderate Inversion

Strong Inversion



* Interestingly\, the product of gm/ID and fT peaks in moderate inversion
* Operating the transistor in moderate inversion is optimal when we value speed and power efficiency equally
  * Not always the case


Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Transit Frequency Chart

![](img/lec-06-gm-ID_2.png)

Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Intrinsic Gain Chart

![](img/lec-06-gm-ID_3.png)

Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Current Density Chart

![](img/lec-06-gm-ID_4.png)

Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Extrinsic Capacitances

Typically OK to work with estimates taken at VDD/2

Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.

# Generic Design Flow



* Determine gm \(from design objectives\)
* Pick L
  * Short channel  high fT \(high speed\)
  * Long channel  high intrinsic gain
* Pick gm/ID \(or fT\)
  * Large gm/ID  low power\, large signal swing\, low VDSsat ≅ 2/\(gm/ID\)
  * Small gm/ID  high fT \(high speed\)
* Determine ID \(from gm and gm/ID\)
* Determine W \(from ID/W\)
* Many other possibilities exist \(depending on circuit specifics\, design constraints and objectives\)


Source: EE315a\, B\. Murmann\, Stanford Univ\.
